title: A Comprehensive Review to Develop & Validate An RP-HPLC Method for Simultaneous Estimation of Anti-Hypertensive Drugs in Bulk & Tablet Dosage Form
author: Mahesh Panjab Lodhe*1, Dr. K. R. Biyani2
license_note: Zenodo record metadata license field: cc-by-4.0
description: Reverse-phase high-performance liquid chromatography (RP-HPLC) has become the analytical technique of choice for the simultaneous estimation of antihypertensive drugs in bulk and pharmaceutical dosage forms due to its superior sensitivity, reproducibility, and precision. This review provides an in-depth analysis of RP-HPLC method development and validation strategies employed in the quantitative determination of commonly co-formulated antihypertensive agents such as amlodipine, losartan, telmisartan, and hydrochlorothiazide. The principles of RP-HPLC, including mobile and stationary phase selection, optimization of chromatographic conditions, and detection techniques, are discussed in detail. Literature data reveal significant variations in mobile phase composition and detection wavelengths, necessitating systematic optimization to achieve adequate resolution and minimal peak tailing. Method validation in accordance with ICH Q2(R1) guidelines ensures reliability and regulatory compliance through evaluation of parameters such as specificity, linearity, accuracy, precision, LOD, LOQ, and robustness. The application of the developed RP-HPLC method to both bulk and tablet dosage forms demonstrates excellent performance, with recovery rates within 98–102% and %RSD values below 2%. Future perspectives highlight the importance of stability-indicating method development, automation, and the transition toward advanced chromatographic techniques such as UPLC and LC-MS. Overall, RP-HPLC remains a cornerstone of modern pharmaceutical quality control, providing a robust analytical framework for the simultaneous estimation of multiple antihypertensive drugs  .

keywords: RP-HPLC, Antihypertensive drugs, Method validation, Fixed-dose combinations, Pharmaceutical analysis
